caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
a: High-mix one-off weldments and repeat production plate parts differ materially in automatable share.
rho: Computerized cutting demonstrates workflow feasibility, not achieved AI substitution.
rho: Small shops may lack sufficient clean historical job data for reliable models.
e: Eligibility is not directly observed.
e: The provided count is margin-bridged and may include low-quality, captive, or cyclically overearning operations.
s5: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures intentions, not completions.
s5: Equipment and customer lists may sell without a transferable operating firm.
q: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
q: Retention differs sharply between repeat OEM production and unique engineered weldments.
d5: Construction is only one portion of plate-work demand and value is not physical tonnage.
d5: Steel prices, tariffs, interest rates, energy cycles, defense budgets, and large-project timing can shift demand rapidly.
o: Production may consolidate into larger operators outside the lens.
o: Automation can reduce labor per job without eliminating accountable operator demand.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Job, project, or production-run quotes for custom plate components and weldments, commonly under repeat OEM and industrial-customer relationships with material, programming, fabrication, inspection, and delivery bundled.
lens.excluded_activities: Steel mills and commodity service centers, captive OEM departments, structural erection contractors, manufacturers outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market plate-work job shops and contract manufacturers repeatedly cutting, punching, bending, rolling, shaping, welding, machining, finishing, inspecting, and assembling purchased metal plate for external industrial, equipment, energy, mining, and construction customers.

a.bridge: Census defines the physical processes and BLS reports occupations for broad fabricated-metal manufacturing, not wage-weighted tasks inside LMM plate-work shops. The range maps high exposure in quoting, drawing review, process planning, nesting, CNC programming, scheduling, purchasing, inspection analysis, documentation, and customer service against low exposure in plate handling, setup, forming, rolling, welding, grinding, finishing, maintenance, and physical inspection; broad BLS data may overstate machining and understate engineering for complex weldments.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
High-mix one-off weldments and repeat production plate parts differ materially in automatable share.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across at least twenty LMM plate shops, split among estimating, engineering, programming, planning, procurement, cutting, forming, rolling, welding, machining, grinding, finishing, inspection, logistics, sales, and administration, with existing automation mapped.
a.rationale: Plate work is physically intensive, and BLS shows welders, assemblers, machinists, and cutting-machine operators as large fabricated-metal occupations. Yet computerized cutting and custom-fabrication workflows create repeatable digital tasks from drawing intake through nesting, programming, quality records, and shipment.

d5.bridge: Census construction put-in-place proxies some plate-work end markets but does not measure constant-quality demand across equipment, energy, mining, industrial maintenance, defense, and construction. Nominal value and megaprojects can overstate physical volume; reshoring and outsourced fabrication share could make broad construction conservative for eligible job shops.
caveats: Construction is only one portion of plate-work demand and value is not physical tonnage.
Steel prices, tariffs, interest rates, energy cycles, defense budgets, and large-project timing can shift demand rapidly.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year shipment and capital-spending forecasts for heavy equipment, energy, mining, process industries, defense, infrastructure, and manufacturing, plus customer forecasts, RFQ counts, backlog, cancellation, utilization, and resourcing data from eligible shops.
d5.rationale: Plate work serves diverse capital-equipment and project markets, supporting a near-flat base but wide cyclical range. Census May 2026 data show total construction down 1.5% year over year and manufacturing construction down 22%, while public highway work rose 2.9%, demonstrating mixed current demand rather than uniform decline.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly observed.
The provided count is margin-bridged and may include low-quality, captive, or cyclically overearning operations.
e.change_evidence: A screened census of the 575 estimated candidates covering external revenue, repeat-order share, contract-manufacturing scope, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, owner dependence, certification, equipment condition, working capital, claims, and environmental exposure.
e.rationale: Plate-work firms commonly operate as outsourced job shops and contract manufacturers producing to external customer specifications, fitting the recurring-service lens despite manufacturing classification. Eligibility is reduced by captive operations, commodity plate processors, one-customer dependence, owner-held technical relationships, obsolete equipment, weak certifications, project-only businesses, and firms outside true normalized EBITDA limits.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332313 ($3,644,691,666) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332313 ($12,213,721,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $282,255 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332313); class <5: 461 firms, avg revenue/firm $580,423 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $113,879 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 304 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,385,605 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $271,856 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 245 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,319,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $651,306 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 481 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,463,210 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,052,882 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,141,585 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,306,179 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $63,273,976 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,414,354 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 575

caveats: Production may consolidate into larger operators outside the lens.
Automation can reduce labor per job without eliminating accountable operator demand.
o.change_evidence: OEM sourcing data on shifts to captive shops, imported fabrications, digital manufacturing networks, self-service ordering, and lights-out production, plus certification and liability requirements for accountable suppliers.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires an accountable operator to interpret drawings, procure and trace plate, select processes, program and maintain equipment, execute qualified welds, inspect dimensions and quality, manage finishing, and stand behind delivery. Software and robotics can automate steps but cannot alone supply certified physical weldments across variable jobs.

caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
Retention differs sharply between repeat OEM production and unique engineered weldments.
q.change_evidence: Matched quote, invoice, and actual-cost data before and after automation, including steel escalators, labor assumptions, discounts, bid hit rate, resourcing events, scrap, rework, claims, lead time, and annual customer repricing.
q.rationale: Fixed quotes and proprietary process knowledge allow near-term retention, especially for complex, urgent, certified, low-volume weldments. Competitive bidding, open-book OEM relationships, material escalators, resourcing, and annual price negotiations pass common productivity gains through. Reduced scrap, faster turnaround, and improved quality should retain better than transparent reductions in quoted labor hours.

caveats: Computerized cutting demonstrates workflow feasibility, not achieved AI substitution.
Small shops may lack sufficient clean historical job data for reliable models.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year pre/post evidence from comparable shops deploying AI for quoting, drawing interpretation, nesting, process planning, scheduling, predictive maintenance, weld and dimensional inspection, and customer service, reporting labor, scrap, rework, throughput, and overrides.
rho.rationale: CAD files, quotes, routings, nesting files, machine logs, weld procedures, inspection results, and delivery data support implementation in estimating, programming, planning, predictive maintenance, and quality support. Constraints include variable drawings, legacy machines, low-volume work, material traceability, qualified welding procedures, cybersecurity, integration costs, and the high cost of scrap or failed weldments.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Discounting plans for internal transfers, closures, failed sales, and equipment-only liquidations yields the base; mature fragmented job-shop ownership could make the proxy conservative, while owner relationships, cyclicality, and specialized equipment can prevent transfer.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures intentions, not completions.
Equipment and customer lists may sell without a transferable operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of NAICS 332313 closed deals, asset liquidations, closures, and owner changes from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, manufacturing brokers, SBA loan files, UCC records, and state registries, matched to eligibility and owner age.
s5.rationale: A large fragmented population should produce regular succession and strategic acquisitions, but completed qualifying control transfers depend on customer retention, management depth, certifications, working capital, equipment, backlog, and environmental diligence.

principal_driver: A large outsourced job-shop base repeats information-heavy quoting and planning work before every physical fabrication job.
principal_weakness: Heavy, variable physical work and customer rebidding constrain both substitution and durable retention.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments manufacturing fabricated metal plate work by cutting, punching, bending, shaping, and welding purchased metal plate.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 332313 Plate Work Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 2025 broader-subsector employment including 125,250 assemblers, 112,520 welders, 101,640 machinists, and 56,060 cutting and press-machine operators.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332

sources.supports: Describes computerized automation, software integration, cutting, beveling, and marking in heavy plate processing, including plate up to 12 inches thick.
sources.title: CNC Plasma and Oxyfuel Plate Cutting Machines

sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports total construction at a $2.210 trillion annual rate, down 1.5% from May 2025; private manufacturing construction down 22.0% and public highway and street construction up 2.9%.
sources.title: Value of Construction Put in Place, May 2026

title: Plate Work Manufacturing
